insights.recommendation: Shift budget towards Referral and Recruiter Outreach channels while reducing spend on LinkedIn.

evidence.key_insight: Referral has the highest ROI with an offer rate of 41.2%, while LinkedIn's offer rate is only 12.5%, indicating a significant inefficiency in spend.

alternative.trade_off: Higher accuracy but greater token cost.

insights.recommendation: Implement salary range disclosure and improve JD clarity to reduce compensation mismatch and early-stage rejections.

evidence.key_insight: Role cancellations and compensation mismatches account for 26.3% of rejections, indicating process failures.

alternative.trade_off: Lower cost but reduced accuracy and depth of analysis.

insights.rag_context:
[1] Recommended actions to reduce avoidable rejections. (1) Add salary range to JD upfront — eliminates most compensation mismatch rejections. (2) Improve Round 1 screening criteria — 20% rejection at R1 suggests JD clarity issue. (3) Implement interviewer calibration sessions — communication and culture fit vary by panel. (4) Add a 24-hour reminder for scheduled interviews — reduces no-shows. (5) Review role cancellation process — 15 cancelled roles wastes candidate pipeline and brand trust. (6) Add an overqualified/underqualified pre-screen question at application stage.

[2] Rejection root cause categorisation. Controllable rejections (team can fix): Role cancelled (15) — headcount planning failure; Compensation mismatch (15) — salary band not communicated early; Communication issues (12) — interviewer training gap; No show (9) — scheduling/engagement failure. Total controllable: 51 rejections (44.7% of all rejections). Candidate-driven rejections: Underqualified (13), Technical skills (11), Overqualified (10), Culture fit (10), Better candidate selected (8). Total: 52 (45.6%). Recommendation: half of all rejections are internally fixable.

[3] Rejection reason breakdown (114 rejections from 200 candidates, 57% rejection rate). Top reasons: Role cancelled (15, 13.2%), Compensation mismatch (15, 13.2%), Underqualified (13, 11.4%), Communication issues (12, 10.5%), Technical skills (11, 9.6%), Background check failed (11, 9.6%), Culture fit (10, 8.8%), Overqualified (10, 8.8%), No show (9, 7.9%), Better candidate selected (8, 7.0%). Role cancellations and compensation mismatches together account for 26.3% — these are process failures, not candidate failures.

[4] Rejection distribution by interview round (stage analysis). Round 1: 23 rejections (20.2%) — high early-stage dropout suggests JD-to-expectation mismatch. Round 2: 31 rejections (27.2%) — largest dropout stage, likely technical screen. Round 3: 21 rejections (18.4%). Round 4: 23 rejections (20.2%). Round 5: 9 rejections (7.9%). Round 6: 7 rejections (6.1%). Benchmark: healthy funnels drop 30-40% at Round 1, 20-25% at Round 2. High Round 4 rejections (20%) relative to Round 3 may indicate panel quality issues late in process.

insights.recommendation: Rebalance panels by moving 12-15 from Sam Rivera to Morgan Chen and Casey Kim immediately.

evidence.key_insight: Sam Rivera is significantly overloaded with 78 panels, well above the safe maximum, risking burnout and scoring inconsistency.

alternative.trade_off: Higher accuracy in recommendations but increased computational cost.

insights.rag_context:
[1] Interviewer overload risk assessment. Industry benchmark: healthy interviewer load is 4-6 interviews per week, or 15-25 per month. Over a hiring cycle, 40-50 total panels is considered sustainable without calibration fatigue. Sam Rivera at 77 panels is 54-93% above safe maximum — high risk of scoring inconsistency. Jamie Osei at 64 is at the upper threshold. Casey Kim and Morgan Chen at 55 are within healthy range. Recommendation: cap any single interviewer at 65 panels per cycle; rebalance Sam Rivera immediately.

[2] Panel rebalancing strategy for overloaded interviewers. To rebalance: move 12-15 panels from Sam Rivera to Casey Kim and Morgan Chen (capacity available). Rotate Quinn Diaz (57) and Jordan Lee (56) into senior-level panels they haven't covered yet. Establish a panel rotation policy: no interviewer should be on more than 3 panels per week. Add a scheduling rule in ATS: flag any interviewer exceeding 70% of average panel load. Cross-train interviewers across role types to improve rebalancing flexibility.

[3] Interviewer panel load distribution (10 interviewers, 200 candidates). Sam Rivera: 77 panels (highest load, 38.5% of candidates). Jamie Osei: 64 panels. Riley Patel: 61. Alex Wang: 61. Drew Singh: 61. Taylor Brooks: 59. Quinn Diaz: 57. Jordan Lee: 56. Morgan Chen: 55. Casey Kim: 55. Average load: 60.6 panels per interviewer. Standard deviation: ~6.5. Sam Rivera is a significant outlier at 27% above average — risk of burnout and inconsistent scoring.

[4] Interview scoring consistency and panel quality benchmarks. Average cumulative round score across all 200 candidates: calculated from data. Score variance increases with interviewer fatigue — overloaded interviewers show wider score ranges. Industry standard: inter-rater reliability (IRR) should be above 0.7 (Cohen's kappa) for technical roles. Best practice: run calibration sessions every 30 panels per interviewer. Panels with 3+ interviewers have better IRR than 1-2 person panels. Recommendation: require minimum 2 interviewers per panel for all senior and above roles.

insights.recommendation: Implement team culture evaluations and reduce time-to-offer to improve acceptance rates.

evidence.key_insight: Team culture concerns are the largest decline reason at 26.1%, and speed-related issues account for 39% of declines.

alternative.trade_off: Lower cost but reduced accuracy and depth in analysis.

insights.rag_context:
[1] Actions to improve offer acceptance rate from 59.6% to benchmark 75%+. (1) Address culture concerns: add team lunch/coffee with potential teammates before offer stage. (2) Reduce time-to-offer: 13% declined due to long timeline — target under 5 days from final interview to offer. (3) Competing offers: implement exploding offer policy with 72-hour decision window for senior roles. (4) Counter-offers: brief candidates on counter-offer likelihood during offer call. (5) Role scope: align JD with actual role scope — 3 declines suggest role was mis-scoped. (6) Compensation: ensure offer is within 5% of candidate's stated expectation gathered at screening.

[2] Offer decline reason breakdown (23 declines). Team culture concerns: 6 (26.1%) — largest category, suggests culture evaluation gap. Competing offer: 3 (13.0%). Timeline too long: 3 (13.0%). Role scope mismatch: 3 (13.0%). Counter-offer accepted: 3 (13.0%). Compensation too low: 2 (8.7%). Personal reasons: 2 (8.7%). Relocation required: 1 (4.3%). Team culture concerns at 26% is unusually high — benchmark is 8-12%. Combined timeline + competing offer + counter-offer = 39% of declines are speed-related.

[3] Offer outcomes summary (57 offers made, 200 candidates total). Offers accepted: 34 (59.6% acceptance rate). Offers declined: 23 (40.4% decline rate). Overall pipeline conversion: 34 hires from 200 candidates = 17% end-to-end conversion. Industry benchmark for offer acceptance: 70-80% for senior engineering roles. Current 59.6% acceptance rate is 10-20 points below benchmark — significant gap requiring action. Each declined offer represents ~60 hours of interview time wasted across panels.

[4] Offer acceptance rates by sourcing channel. Company Website converts best once an offer is made: 85.7% acceptance rate (6 of 7 offers). Glassdoor is second at 77.8% (7 of 9). AngelList and LinkedIn both sit at 50.0%. Hackathon is the weakest closer at 40.0% — candidates from events may be passively exploring. Benchmark: industry average offer acceptance is 70-75%; channels below 55% warrant re-evaluation.

insights.recommendation: Implement weekly pipeline reviews and automated nudges for hiring managers to address SLA breaches.

evidence.key_insight: Multiple roles are breaching SLA, indicating a need for improved pipeline management.

alternative.trade_off: Higher accuracy in predictions but increased processing time.

insights.rag_context:
[1] Time-to-hire SLA benchmarks by position level. Mid-level roles: SLA target 21-28 days from application to offer. Senior roles: SLA target 28-35 days. Lead roles: SLA target 35-42 days. Staff roles: SLA target 35-45 days. Principal roles: SLA target 42-55 days. Average rounds by level in this dataset: Lead 3.32, Senior 3.01, Mid 2.84, Principal 2.54, Staff 2.39. Staff has fewer rounds but longer calendar time due to scheduling complexity. Every reschedule adds 3-5 days; 50% of candidates had 2+ reschedules — expect pipeline to run long.

[2] Pipeline velocity benchmarks for technical hiring. Industry average time-to-hire by role: Software Engineer 35-45 days, Senior Engineer 42-52 days, Staff/Principal 55-70 days, Data Scientist 40-55 days. Interview-to-offer conversion benchmark: 35-45% (current dataset: 28.5% — below benchmark). Offer-to-acceptance conversion benchmark: 72-78% (current: 59.6% — below benchmark). Sourcing-to-screen conversion: typically 20-30% of applicants reach phone screen. Pipeline health score formula: (Offer rate × Acceptance rate) / SLA compliance × 100.

[3] Pipeline bottleneck analysis. Round 2 has highest rejection rate (31 rejections, 27.2%) — technical screen is primary filter. If Round 2 is async/take-home: high dropout may indicate poor candidate experience, not skill gap. Rescheduling is a significant bottleneck: 51% of candidates had 2-3 reschedules, adding 6-15 days to average time-to-hire. Role cancellations (15) mid-pipeline waste 15+ candidate-slots and damage employer brand. Recommendation: freeze headcount approval must precede JD posting, not follow it.

[4] SLA breach indicators and escalation thresholds. Amber alert: role open for 80% of SLA with no offer stage reached. Red alert: role breaches SLA with no accepted offer. Common SLA breach causes: interviewer unavailability (reschedules), delayed debrief meetings, slow hiring manager response on offer approval. Roles with 3+ reschedules are 2.4x more likely to breach SLA. Benchmark: SLA breach rate should be under 15% of open roles. Recommended action: weekly pipeline review for all roles past 60% of SLA target. Automated nudges to hiring managers at 70% and 85% of SLA window.

suggestions: sourcing_quality latency is 9705ms — consider prompt compression to reduce tokens.
